story:
Hazel held a forked willow out in front of her by the ends while ten-year-old Molly trailed after, their steps swishing through yellowing knee-high grass under a full harvest moon. The stick quivered, then twisted like a cat reaching for the ground. “This is for show,” Hazel said. “Folk like to see something happen. But you don’t need the stick, understand?” Molly nodded, watching wisps of fair hair escape Hazel’s braid and catch the moonlight, bright enough to cast shadows. If Molly stood just so, the moonlight softened her aunt’s face, shining on her crooked front tooth and sweet, clear blue eyes—eyes Molly’s mama had too, though Molly’s own were brown, like her father’s.

“What really happens,” Hazel said, “happens inside you. You’ve got to feel the earth. She’s got warm places and wet places, soft and hard places. You can feel the water in her, feel it in yourself. Your feet feel damp and cool, even in your shoes, and then you know. The wetter your feet feel, the closer the water.” She spun Molly once, steadied her, and set the willow in her hands. “Open your eyes, but don’t look too hard. Just walk and feel.” Molly tried. Wherever she went, nothing happened. In Hazel’s hands the willow arched near to breaking; in Molly’s it hung dead as a broom. “Never mind,” Hazel said, kissing her and smoothing her sleek brown hair. “We’ll try again. There’s always a water witch in this family.”

They never tried again. Two days later Hazel cut herself canning. The wound sickened; red streaks crept up her arm. Nothing helped. She died at the dark of the moon, when life goes out of things and death comes easy. They buried her on the rise at the back of the farm, in the small family graveyard where her grandparents and parents lay, and the brother who had died a baby. After, Molly took the forked willow, drying though it was, and walked the field day after day, trying to find the place where Hazel’s fresh-cut switch had twisted toward water. She knew it was foolish. A real water witch didn’t need a stick, and no stick could make one.

When the full moon rose again, Molly climbed to the graveyard. The air was blue and chill with fall; leaves made a rustling carpet. She laid the willow on Hazel’s grave. “I couldn’t do it,” she whispered. “I tried and tried. I’m sorry, Aunt Hazel. I’m sorry we don’t have a water witch in the family now.” She cried as hard for her failure as for her aunt.

Turning for home, she watched the moon float before her and wondered where she might stand to make a halo for herself. Halfway across the field a breeze touched her cheek and crept through her hair; she shivered and hurried on. Then for a moment the breeze was a warm breath. “Aunt Hazel?” she breathed, longing to turn, afraid it wouldn’t be true. A chill pricked her left foot through the wool sock. Cold spread over her sole and toes. She bent, tugged off her shoe. The sock sagged, dripping cold, clear water.
